content.full_text:
CONTRA COSTA COUNTY EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT ASSOCIATION
THE WILLOWS OFFICE PARK
1355 WILLOW WAY, SUITE 221
CONCORD, CALIFORNIA 94520
(415) 646-5741
FAX: (415) 646-5747

January 22, 1990


Ms. Linda Moureaux
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Moureaux:

As requested, this letter serves to formally request your agency's advice
regarding Government Code Section 87200 as recently changed by Chapter
403.

Specifically my questions relate to the requirement that Form 721 be filed
by "other public officials who manage public investments," and how this
applies to this Association's money managers.

In a memorandum, dated November 7, 1989, to County Counsel, the FPPC
expanded the definition of "other public officials" to include
"consultants who have decision making authority . . .". I was informed by
our County Counsel that a "consultant" is further defined by the FPPC to
be a "natural person who provides, under contract, information, advice,
recommendations or counsel . . .".

The County's Retirement Board retains money managers to invest the assets
of the pension trust. In doing so, it specifies the amount of funds for
which the manager is to be responsible, the asset class in which the
manager may invest (stocks, bonds, domestic, global, etc.), the objective
rate of return expected, the benchmark index against which performance
will be measured, and any other limitation on the Manager's discretion.

The decision as to how much of its total portfolio should go into any one
asset class is solely the Board's. The Board reviews managers'
performance quarterly at a minimum. The decision to hire or fire a
manager is solely the Board's. Within the parameters set forth in the
contract, managers are given sole discretion as to the timing and
selection of securities trades. All trades are reported daily to the
Association office.

My review of the pertinent law, the FPPC guidelines given to date, and my
knowledge of the contractual arrangement between the Board and its money

Ms. Linda Moureaux
January 22, 1990
Page 2


managers, leads me to conclude that our money managers are not
"consultants":

- Our money managers are firms, not "natural persons".

- They're retained, under contract, to buy and sell securities not to
provide information, advice, recommendations or counsel.

- They're retained for their investment style and, while their
performance may be indirectly affected, they are not judged on their
market timing or trade activities.

- We evaluate money managers on their ability to meet or exceed return
objectives while adhering to the investment style for which they were
retained.

- All proxy votes are cast by the Board.

- No assets, except for short term cash to facilitate trades, are held
by the money manager, but rather by the Association's custodian bank.

- No one individual at the money manager firm is solely responsible for
decisions related to our portion of their total portfolio (see
Attachment A).

- All money managers (firms) are required to register their activities
with the SEC on form ADV, "Uniform Application for Investment Advisor
Registration". This form is extensive in its information
requirements (see Attachment B). Part II of this form is annually
provided to the Association by each manager and available to the
general public.

Finally, if it is determined that our money manager firms are "public
officials" as used in Government Code Section 87200, I request guidance on
who at the firm is to file.

As is noted on Attachment A, and if everyone employed by the firm needs to
file, the FPPC could receive over 1000 forms from Contra Costa managers
alone. If every public pension plan in California does likewise, this
figure can easily be multiplied by 20 or 30, since each of us has from one
to ten (or more) money managers and they're not the same firms.

Ms. Linda Moureaux
January 22, 1990
Page 3


If these firms are required to file, I believe the FPPC's intent could be
easily and efficiently served by obtaining copies of SEC Form ADV from the
money managers, from the pension plans or directly from the SEC.

I await your reply before contacting any money managers on this filing
requirement.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Patricia F. Wiegert, CEBS
Retirement Administrator

PW/tw

Attachment A
Attachment B

cc: County Counsel w/attachments

ATTACHMENT A


TYPICAL ORGANIZATION OF
MONEY MANAGER FIRM
UNDER CONTRACT WITH CONTRA COSTA
COUNTY EMPLOYEES' RETIREMENT ASSOCIATION


The firm can employ anywhere from three or four people to over 1,000
people.

In addition to the usual CEO and management principals, the firm might
employ economists, research analysts, marketing representatives, graphic
artists, securities traders, accountants, and clerical personnel.

The Board contracts with money managers who then follow either an active
or a passive strategy per contract.

"ACTIVE" MANAGEMENT

Investment decisions are made by a variety of people: the CEO may develop
overall firm strategy, a committee may determine sector selection,
weighting and duration decisions may be influenced by economists or
researchers, individual bond issues or company stock selections are made,
perhaps by a group, within the parameters set by other decision-makers.

In the end a transaction (buy, sell) will take place based on all the
previous decisions, and perhaps also influenced by market movements and
strike price.

Trades are usually done in blocks.  Whether or not a portion of that block
is then attributed to our portfolio or some other client portfolio is yet
another decision.

"PASSIVE" MANAGEMENT

This style of investment replicates a pre-selected index such as the S & P
500. Portfolios hold either a portion of or the entire index, or a
selection of securities which serve as a proxy to the index. The
objective is to duplicate whatever the index returns. Trades are done to
periodically rebalance the portfolio to the index.

ATTACHMENT B


INFORMATION REPORTED ON SEC FORM ADV


Types of Services

Fee Structure

Types of Clients

Types of Investments

Methods of Analysis

Sources of Information

Investment Strategies

Education and Business Background of:
  - Investment committee members
  - Supervisor of individuals who give advice
  - Principal executive officers

Other Business Activities

Other Financial Industry Activities or Affiliations

Participation or Interest in Client Transactions

Conditions for Managing Accounts

Review of Accounts: Nature, Frequency, Content

Investment or Brokerage Discretion

Other Compensation

Balance Sheet if Firm has Custody of Client Funds

ATTACHMENT A

Types of Clients

Types of Investments

Methods of Analysis

Sources of Information

Education and Business Background of:
  - Investment committee members
  - Supervisor of individuals who give advice
  - Principal executive officers

Other Business Activities

Other Financial Industry Activities or Affiliations

Participation or Interest in Client Transactions

Conditions for Managing Accounts

Review of Accounts: Nature, Frequency, Content

Investment or Brokerage Discretion

Other Compensation

Balance Sheet if Firm has Custody of Client Funds

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 31, 1990


Patricia Wiegert
Contra Costa County Employees'
Retirement Association
1355 Willow Way, Suite 221
Concord, CA 94520

Re: Letter No. 90-074

Dear Ms. Wiegert:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on January 26, 1990 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact Blanca Breeze an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed. If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can. (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

[illegible]

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

KED:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

sections.question: My review of the pertinent law, the FPPC Guidelines given to date, and my knowledge of the contractual arrangement between the Board and its money managers, leads me to conclude that our money managers are not 'consultants' and therefore not required to file Form 721 as 'other public officials who manage public investments.' Does the FPPC agree with this interpretation? Additionally, if the FPPC determines that money manager firms are 'public officials' under Government Code Section 87200, who specifically at the firm would be required to file?
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks whether money manager firms retained by Contra Costa County Employees' Retirement Association to invest pension assets are required to file Form 721 as 'other public officials' under Government Code Section 87200, and if so, which individuals at those firms would need to file.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC dated January 22, 1990. The FPPC's acknowledgment letter dated January 31, 1990 is included but contains no substantive response yet. The document includes detailed attachments explaining money manager firm structure and SEC Form ADV requirements. Question and conclusion are synthesized from the body of the request letter as no explicit Q/A sections are present.

Does the FPPC agree with this interpretation? Additionally, if the FPPC determines that money manager firms are 'public officials' under Government Code Section 87200, who specifically at the firm would be required to file?

embedding.summary: Contra Costa County Employees' Retirement Association requests FPPC advice on whether external money manager firms managing pension assets constitute 'other public officials' requiring Form 721 filing under Government Code Section 87200, arguing they should not be classified as consultants given their organizational structure, SEC registration, and limited decision-making authority within contractually specified parameters.
